details.text: Derivative: الْمَجِيدِ means full of majesty. مَجْد (Majd) refers to something that demands respect, glory and grandeur which people should be in awe of.
Derivative: نَضِيدٌ means layered; one layer on top of another.
Derivative: بَاسِقَاتٍ in reference to palm trees that are tall and full of dates.
Derivative: الرَّسِّ means the deep well.
Derivative: وَعِيدِ from وَعَدَ which means to promise. وَعْدٌ (Wa’d) means a promise and وَعِيدٌ means a promise with threatening consequences attached to it or simply a threat i.e., of punishment.

Derivative: لَبْسٍ (Labs) here means اِلْتِباس (Iltibaas) confusion and be able to distinguish or discern. فِي لَبْسٍ means in a state of confusion i.e., by holding contradictory or inconsistent positions regarding Allah’s ability to create and then recreate.
Derivative: عَيِينَا from عَيَّ which means to lack the strength, power or capability.

Derivative: الْوَرِيدِ when attached to حَبْلِ (Habl – A rope) it refers to the jugular vein. وَريد comes from وَرَد which means to come to a water source. It is called حَبْلِ الْوَرِيدِ because firstly it resembles a rope and secondly because if it is cut blood gushes out like water gushes out of a fountain or stream.
Derivative: رَقِيبٌ refers to someone who watches over, supervises and acts as a guardian.
Derivative: عَتِيدٌ means ready and present i.e., to record someone’s actions at all times.
Derivative: يَلْفِظُ means to pronounce or utter.
Derivative: أُزْلِفَتِ from أَزْلَفَ means to be brought down and lowered towards something, to be brought close and near.
Derivative: حَفِيظٍ means someone who guards and preserves i.e., the one who guards over and maintains what Allah has commanded him with and the trust that has he has been charged with.
Derivative: نَقَّبُوا from نَقَّبَ. نَقَّبَ means to travel, explore and search. In this case they were searching for a place to escape to (مَحِيصٍ) from the fear of death.
- أَلْقَى السَّمْعَ literally means to “throw the hearing” which means that he paid attention or listened attentively.

- السُّجُودِ (Sujood) is the last main act of the prayer and أَدْبَارَ the plural of دُبْر refers to the last part, back or end of something. The phrase therefore means at the end of the Sujood (i.e., prayer) or upon its ending.
Derivative: تَشَقَّقُ means to split and crack open.
Derivative: سِرَاعًا means in a rush or rapidly or here referring to those who come out from their graves, that they will come out in a rush.

Derivative: مَرِيجٍ means when you mix or merge things together and you cannot tell the difference between them and hence it becomes confusing. Here it is referencing the polytheists inconsistent and confused accusations.
Derivative: مُبَارَكًا means full of the power of Barakah (بَرَكَةٌ). The quality of Barakah (بَرَكَةٌ) is that it brings out goodness from something’s potential.
